content.text: Procuring an LLM vendor is not the same as procuring a SaaS tool. When prompts flow through an external API, they carry the organisation's intellectual property, potentially its customers' personal data, and the logic of its proprietary systems. A vendor that trains on customer prompts by default, retains logs indefinitely, or cannot produce a current SOC 2 Type II report introduces risk that no enterprise security team will accept after the fact. The due diligence process must happen before contract signature, not after the first incident.
Why LLM Due Diligence Is Different

Standard software vendor due diligence covers security certifications, uptime SLAs, and data processing agreements. LLM vendor due diligence adds three categories that have no direct equivalent in conventional SaaS procurement:
Training data use.

Conventional SaaS vendors process your data to deliver a service; they do not improve their core product using it. Many LLM providers have historically used API prompts as training data by default, with opt-out requiring explicit contract terms. Whether and how the vendor uses customer data to train or fine-tune models is a deal-breaker question that must be answered in writing.
Model versioning and deprecation.

A SaaS vendor updates their application; the API contract stays stable. An LLM vendor updates the underlying model; the application's behaviour changes even if the code is identical. The diligence process must establish whether specific model versions can be pinned, how much notice is given before a model is deprecated, and what the contractual obligation is to maintain a working version.
Output liability and indemnification.

If a model produces output that infringes copyright or causes harm to an end user, who is liable? The answer differs significantly across vendors and must be addressed in the contract. Some vendors provide limited indemnification for IP infringement in model outputs under specific conditions; others do not.
Domain 1: Data Handling
Data handling is the highest-stakes domain because mistakes are often irreversible. A prompt log that has been used for training cannot be un-trained on after the fact.
Requirement
Acceptable vendor answer
Red flag
Training on customer prompts
Opt-out by default; contract clause confirming no use of customer data for training
"We may use your data to improve our services" without an opt-out mechanism
Data residency
Explicit region options; contractual commitment to stay within specified region
"We are a global service" with no region controls
Prompt log retention
Configurable retention ≤ 30 days; deletion on request; zero-retention option available
Indefinite retention for debugging purposes; no deletion SLA
Cross-tenant isolation
Cryptographic isolation; dedicated tenancy option for regulated workloads
"Logical isolation" without technical controls described
Sub-processors
Published list of all sub-processors with region and purpose; advance notice of changes
No published sub-processor list; sub-processor changes without notice
Encryption
AES-256 at rest; TLS 1.2+ in transit; key management documentation provided
Encryption described as "industry standard" without specifics

The data processing agreement (DPA) should cite GDPR Article 28 for EU customers and include specific obligations for processor conduct. A DPA that merely references "applicable data protection law" without specifics is insufficient for regulated industries. Legal review of the DPA is not optional.
Domain 2: Security Posture

Security certifications are evidence, not guarantees. A SOC 2 Type II report from a reputable auditor, completed within the last 12 months, is the minimum bar. The report should cover the system description relevant to the LLM API service, not just the vendor's corporate infrastructure.
Certification / control
What to verify
Minimum standard
SOC 2 Type II
Request the full report; check audit period (must be recent), auditor, and scope of systems covered
Report < 12 months old; LLM API in scope; no critical exceptions unaddressed
Certificate and accreditation body; scope statement must include cloud services
Certificate < 3 years old; renewal audits documented
Penetration testing
Request executive summary of most recent pen test; check date and scope; ask about critical findings and remediation
External pen test within 12 months; critical findings remediated before next engagement
Vulnerability disclosure
Published responsible disclosure policy; ask for average time-to-patch for critical CVEs
Public bug bounty or coordinated disclosure program; critical CVE patch < 30 days
Incident notification
Ask for the contractual notification SLA for security incidents affecting customer data
72-hour notification for breaches as required by GDPR; 24-hour for critical incidents in enterprise contracts
Access controls
Ask whether vendor employees have access to customer prompts; under what conditions; how access is logged
Access to customer data restricted to specific roles; all access logged; access reviews quarterly
Domain 3: Compliance

Compliance requirements depend on the industry and geography of the deploying organisation. The due diligence process must identify which compliance frameworks apply before evaluating the vendor against them.
HIPAA

If prompts will include protected health information (PHI) — even indirectly, as part of clinical decision support or patient communication — a signed Business Associate Agreement (BAA) is required before any PHI is sent to the vendor. The BAA must:
Name the covered entity and business associate explicitly.
Describe permitted uses of PHI.
Require the vendor to report breaches within the HIPAA-required timeframe.
Commit to deletion of PHI upon contract termination.
Not all LLM providers offer BAAs. Providers that do not offer a BAA cannot be used for any workload that may involve PHI, even in prompt metadata.
EU AI Act

The EU AI Act (in force from August 2024, with phased compliance deadlines) classifies AI systems by risk level. General-purpose AI models used via API are subject to transparency obligations for the provider. The deploying organisation must ensure:
The vendor can demonstrate compliance with Article 53 transparency requirements for general-purpose AI models.
If the system is used in a high-risk context (hiring, credit scoring, medical devices, law enforcement), the deploying organisation takes on additional obligations as an AI system deployer.
Technical documentation and conformity declarations are available for enterprise customers.
Export Controls

Some LLM providers use cloud infrastructure located in or routing through jurisdictions with export control implications. If your organisation operates under ITAR or EAR restrictions, confirm that the vendor's infrastructure and any sub-processors comply with applicable export control law. This is particularly relevant for defence, aerospace, and dual-use technology companies.
Domain 4: Reliability

An LLM vendor SLA of 99.9% uptime sounds acceptable until you calculate what it means: 8.7 hours of downtime per year, which is 8.7 hours during which any service depending on the model API is degraded or unavailable. For production use cases, the relevant questions are not just the headline SLA but the credit structure, the maintenance window policy, and whether there is a multi-region failover option.
Reliability dimension
Question to ask
Minimum acceptable answer
Uptime SLA
What is the contractual uptime commitment, and how is it measured?
≥ 99.9%; measured as successful API responses / total requests; excludes scheduled maintenance
SLA credit
What credit is issued when SLA is breached?
Prorated credits based on downtime duration; credits applied without requiring customer claim
Rate limits
What are the default rate limits, and can they be increased contractually?
Published rate limit tiers; enterprise tier with guaranteed throughput available
Model version pinning
Can a specific model version be pinned, and for how long?
Pinning supported; minimum 6-month notice before version deprecation; longer in enterprise contracts
Maintenance windows
When does maintenance occur, and is advance notice provided?
Maintenance outside business hours (specify timezone); minimum 48-hour advance notice; no maintenance during agreed blackout periods
Multi-region
Is multi-region deployment available for business continuity?
At least 2 geographic regions; failover documented; customer can specify preferred region
The Due Diligence Questionnaire

Send a structured questionnaire to vendor candidates before any technical evaluation. The questionnaire elicits the information needed to complete the five domain assessments. Vendors who cannot or will not answer the questionnaire are disqualified without proceeding to a technical trial.

# LLM Vendor Security Due Diligence Questionnaire

**Vendor name:**
**Product/service:**
**Questionnaire version:** 2026-Q2
**Responder name and title:**

---

## Section 1: Data Handling

1. Do you use customer API prompts, completions, or usage data to train
   or fine-tune your models? If yes, is there an opt-out mechanism?
   What are the contractual terms?

2. Where is customer data stored and processed? List all geographic regions.
   Is data guaranteed to remain within a specified region if requested?

3. What is your default prompt log retention period?
   Can customers configure a shorter retention period or zero retention?
   What is your process for deleting customer data on request?

4. Describe how customer tenants are isolated from each other.
   Is there a dedicated tenancy option (no shared compute)?

5. Provide a complete list of sub-processors that have access to customer data,
   including their location and purpose.

## Section 2: Security

6. Provide your most recent SOC 2 Type II report or a summary letter
   from your auditor confirming no material exceptions. What is the audit period
   and which systems are in scope?

7. When was your most recent third-party penetration test conducted?
   Were critical findings identified? If so, what was the remediation timeline?

8. What is your vulnerability disclosure policy?
   What is your typical time-to-patch for critical security vulnerabilities?

9. Under what circumstances do vendor employees have access to customer prompts?
   How is this access logged and reviewed?

10. What is your contractual notification SLA if a security incident
    affects customer data?

## Section 3: Compliance

11. Do you offer a HIPAA Business Associate Agreement (BAA)?
    If so, describe the scope and any conditions.

12. Have you completed an EU AI Act compliance assessment?
    Can you share your AI Act technical documentation or conformity declaration?

13. Are you FedRAMP authorized or in the FedRAMP authorization process?
    (Required for U.S. federal government use cases.)

## Section 4: Reliability and Model Versioning

14. What is your contractual uptime SLA? How is uptime measured?
    What credits are issued for SLA breaches?

15. Can customers pin a specific model version? For how long?
    What advance notice is provided before a model version is deprecated?

16. What are your default API rate limits?
    What enterprise tier options exist for higher throughput?

## Section 5: Exit and Portability

17. If we terminate the contract, how do we export our data
    (fine-tuning datasets, usage logs, prompt templates)?
    What is the export window before data is deleted?

18. Provide written confirmation that all customer data is deleted
    within 30 days of contract termination, with a certificate of deletion
    available on request.

19. Is your API compatible with OpenAI's API interface?
    Do you have a migration guide for moving workloads between providers?
Scoring and Decision

After receiving the vendor's questionnaire responses, the due diligence team scores each domain on a 1–5 scale. A score of 1 in any domain is an automatic disqualification regardless of the composite score — it indicates a critical gap that the vendor cannot address contractually.
Score
Meaning
Implication
Fully satisfactory; evidence provided; contractual commitment available
No conditions on proceeding
Mostly satisfactory; minor gaps with acceptable mitigations
Document the gap and mitigation; proceed
Partially satisfactory; contractual remediation required
Negotiate contract clause; track as obligation; re-evaluate at renewal
Significant gap; compensating controls may be possible
Legal and security sign-off required; executive escalation if proceeding
Critical gap; no acceptable mitigation available
Disqualify vendor; do not proceed

Composite score ≥ 20/25 with no domain score below 3 is the threshold to proceed to contract negotiation. Scores between 15–19 require executive approval. Scores below 15 indicate the vendor should be disqualified unless a critical business need outweighs the risk, which itself requires board-level approval.
Contract Negotiation Priorities

The due diligence assessment feeds directly into contract negotiation. Every domain score below 5 is a negotiation objective. The following clauses are the highest-priority asks for enterprise LLM contracts:
Data and Privacy Clauses

# Priority contract clauses — data handling

1. **No-training clause (mandatory)**
   "Vendor shall not use Customer data, including prompts, completions,
   usage logs, or fine-tuning datasets, to train, fine-tune, or otherwise
   improve Vendor's models or services, either directly or through
   Vendor's third-party sub-processors."

2. **Data residency commitment**
   "Customer data shall be processed and stored exclusively within the
   [EU / US / specified region]. Vendor shall not transfer Customer data
   outside this region without Customer's prior written consent."

3. **Prompt log retention cap**
   "Vendor shall retain prompt logs for no longer than [30 days] from
   the date of generation. Vendor shall delete all prompt logs upon
   Customer's written request within [5 business days]."

4. **Sub-processor change notice**
   "Vendor shall provide [30 days] written notice before adding or
   replacing any sub-processor. Customer may object within [15 days].
   If Customer objects and the parties cannot resolve the objection,
   Customer may terminate for cause."

5. **Deletion on termination**
   "Within [30 days] of contract termination, Vendor shall delete all
   Customer data from all systems, including backups, and provide a
   written certificate of deletion within [10 business days] of request."
Model Versioning and Reliability Clauses

# Priority contract clauses — model and reliability

6. **Model version pinning and deprecation notice**
   "Upon Customer's request, Vendor shall support access to a specific
   model version for a minimum of [12 months] from the date of Customer's
   first production use of that version. Vendor shall provide [90 days]
   written notice before deprecating any model version in use by Customer."

7. **Uptime SLA with financial remedy**
   "Vendor commits to [99.9%] monthly uptime. For each [0.1%] below
   the committed uptime, Vendor shall credit Customer's account [10%]
   of the monthly fee for the affected service tier. Credits are applied
   automatically; Customer need not submit a claim."

8. **IP indemnification**
   "Vendor shall defend and indemnify Customer against third-party claims
   that outputs generated by Vendor's models, when used as permitted under
   this Agreement, infringe third-party intellectual property rights,
   subject to the following conditions: [usage restrictions, geographic
   scope, carve-outs for customer-provided fine-tuning data]."

9. **Throughput guarantee**
   "Vendor shall process Customer API requests at a minimum rate of
   [N tokens/minute] during business hours. Sustained throttling below
   this rate constitutes a service credit event under clause [X]."
Exit and Audit Rights
Right to audit.

Enterprise contracts should include the right to audit the vendor's security controls annually, either directly or through a qualified third-party auditor. Vendors may substitute a fresh SOC 2 Type II report in lieu of a customer-directed audit.
Termination for convenience.

Ensure the contract allows termination without cause with a reasonable notice period (90 days is typical for enterprise SaaS). LLM vendor lock-in is a real risk; avoid long-term contracts without a termination-for-convenience clause.
Data portability.

Negotiate a clause requiring the vendor to provide a complete export of all customer-owned data (fine-tuning datasets, usage analytics, prompt templates if stored) in a standard format (JSON, CSV, or equivalent) within 30 days of contract termination.
Change-in-control clause.

If the vendor is acquired, the acquiring entity should be required to honour the existing contract terms for a minimum of 24 months. This is particularly important for privacy commitments — a no-training clause agreed with a privacy-forward vendor should survive an acquisition by a less privacy-focused parent.
Ongoing Vendor Monitoring

Signing the contract is not the end of due diligence — it is the beginning of a relationship that requires ongoing monitoring. Vendor security posture, compliance status, and data handling practices can change between annual reviews.
Monitoring activity
Cadence
Owner
Action trigger
SOC 2 Type II report review
Annual (on renewal)
Security team
New material exceptions → remediation plan required; critical exceptions → escalate to executive sponsor
Pen test report review
Annual
Security team
Unremediated critical findings → notify vendor; if unresolved in 30 days, evaluate alternative vendors
Sub-processor list review
Quarterly
Privacy team / Legal
New sub-processor in sensitive jurisdiction → evaluate data flow impact; invoke objection right if needed
Incident notification tracking
Continuous
Security operations
Any breach notification → activate incident response plan; evaluate impact to customer data
Model deprecation notices
Continuous
Platform team
Deprecation notice received → initiate migration planning; ensure successor model has been evaluated
SLA breach reporting
Monthly
Platform team
SLA breach → claim credit automatically; multiple consecutive breaches → vendor performance review meeting
Regulatory update scan
Quarterly
Legal
New compliance requirement applicable to vendor → issue questionnaire update; verify vendor compliance

The vendor monitoring checklist is included in the platform team's quarterly operational review. A vendor that accumulates three or more monitoring flags without resolution enters the vendor risk review process, which may result in migration to an alternative vendor.
Multi-Vendor Strategy to Mitigate Lock-in

Dependency on a single LLM vendor concentrates risk: a vendor outage, a pricing change, or a capability regression can affect all production workloads simultaneously. A multi-vendor strategy — routing different workloads to different vendors, or maintaining a warm failover — distributes that risk.
Routing by Workload Type

Different models from different vendors have different strength profiles. A multi-vendor routing strategy assigns workloads to the model best suited for the task, and incidentally distributes vendor dependency:
Long-context summarisation → vendor A (strong context window, competitive pricing per token)
Code generation and review → vendor B (strong coding benchmark scores, available on-premises)
Customer-facing chat → vendor A (lowest latency, best instruction following)
Complex reasoning and planning → vendor C (strongest reasoning, acceptable for asynchronous workloads)
LLM Gateway as Abstraction Layer

The LLM gateway in the platform architecture provides the abstraction layer that makes multi-vendor routing operational without requiring application changes. Application code calls the gateway's unified API; the gateway routes to the appropriate vendor based on routing rules configured per model name.

# gateway/router.py
# Route model requests to the appropriate vendor based on config.

from typing import Literal

# Routing table: model alias → (vendor, actual model ID)
ROUTING_TABLE: dict[str, tuple[str, str]] = {
    "text-summary-v1":  ("bedrock",   "anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022-v2:0"),
    "code-review-v1":   ("azure",     "gpt-4o"),
    "chat-fast-v1":     ("anthropic", "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"),
    "reasoning-v1":     ("bedrock",   "anthropic.claude-opus-4-8"),
    # Fallback: if primary vendor is degraded, route to secondary
    "text-summary-v1:fallback": ("azure", "gpt-4o"),
}

VENDOR_CLIENTS = {
    "bedrock":   BedrockClient(),
    "azure":     AzureOpenAIClient(),
    "anthropic": AnthropicClient(),
}

def route_request(model_alias: str, prompt: str, use_fallback: bool = False) -> str:
    key = f"{model_alias}:fallback" if use_fallback else model_alias
    vendor, model_id = ROUTING_TABLE[key]
    client = VENDOR_CLIENTS[vendor]
    return client.complete(model_id=model_id, prompt=prompt)

The routing table is the only place where vendor identity appears. Switching from one vendor to another for a workload requires updating one line in the routing table — not changing application code. This is the practical value of the gateway abstraction for multi-vendor strategy.
Summary

LLM vendor due diligence spans five domains: data handling (training use, residency, retention), security posture (SOC 2, pen tests, access controls), compliance (HIPAA, EU AI Act, export controls), reliability (SLA, rate limits, model pinning), and exit risk (data portability, lock-in). A structured questionnaire sent before any technical evaluation elicits the evidence needed to score each domain. Critical gaps — vendors that train on customer prompts without opt-out, lack current security certifications, or cannot commit to model version retention — are disqualifiers regardless of other strengths. The due diligence output is a scored assessment that the legal team uses to negotiate contract terms and that the security team uses to approve the vendor for production use.
